title: GitHub Review by greptile-apps[bot]
summary:
Two issues found:

1. **`verify_python_base.sh` has the same `set -e` bug already fixed in `verify_venv_builder.sh`**: the `docker run` exit code is never captured because `set -e` aborts the script before `$?` is read. Fix by using the `|| exit_code=$?` idiom.

2. **Doc build commands in `container-images.md` use wrong tag names**: tags like `c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble` don't match the bare `FROM python-base` directives in downstream Dockerfiles, so following the docs causes build failures. Tags must use the bare names (`-t python-base`, `-t venv-builder`).

code_comments.body:
`set -e` aborts script before exit code is captured — identical bug to the one fixed in `verify_venv_builder.sh`

With `set -euo pipefail` active, if `docker run` returns a non-zero exit code, bash exits immediately when evaluating `version=$(docker run ...)` — before `local exit_code=$?` on the next line is ever reached. The test never records a FAIL; it just silently aborts the script.

This is exactly the pattern that was identified and fixed in `verify_venv_builder.sh`. The same fix needs to be applied here.

```suggestion
  local version exit_code=0
  version=$(docker run --rm "$IMAGE" python3 --version 2>&1) || exit_code=$?
```

The `|| exit_code=$?` idiom prevents `set -e` from triggering because the command is part of a `||` list, so the overall list succeeds while still capturing the real exit code.

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown
This is a comment left during a code review.
Path: tests/container-images/verify_python_base.sh
Line: 26-27

Comment:
`set -e` aborts script before exit code is captured — identical bug to the one fixed in `verify_venv_builder.sh`

With `set -euo pipefail` active, if `docker run` returns a non-zero exit code, bash exits immediately when evaluating `version=$(docker run ...)` — before `local exit_code=$?` on the next line is ever reached. The test never records a FAIL; it just silently aborts the script.

This is exactly the pattern that was identified and fixed in `verify_venv_builder.sh`. The same fix needs to be applied here.

```suggestion
  local version exit_code=0
  version=$(docker run --rm "$IMAGE" python3 --version 2>&1) || exit_code=$?
```

The `|| exit_code=$?` idiom prevents `set -e` from triggering because the command is part of a `||` list, so the overall list succeeds while still capturing the real exit code.

How can I resolve this? If you propose a fix, please make it concise.
`````
</details>
code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Fixed in verify_python_base.sh: replaced the three-line pattern (local version / version=$(...) / local exit_code=$?) with the set -e-safe two-line pattern (local version exit_code=0 / version=$(...) || exit_code=$?) exactly as suggested by the reviewer.

code_comments.body:
Build commands produce wrong tag names — `docker build images/venv-builder` would fail as written

The build commands tag the image as `c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble`, but `images/venv-builder/Dockerfile` begins with `FROM python-base` (not `FROM c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble`). Following these instructions exactly creates an image named `c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble` locally, then `docker build images/venv-builder` tries to resolve `FROM python-base` → Docker looks for `python-base:latest` which doesn't exist, causing the build to fail.

The same problem applies to the Keystone Stage 1 and Stage 2 (`FROM python-base`, `FROM venv-builder`).

The note correctly states "The tag names (`python-base`, `venv-builder`) must match the `FROM` directives" — but the preceding code block contradicts that. The commands need to use the bare tag names that the `FROM` directives reference:

```suggestion
# Build python-base (tag must match FROM python-base in downstream Dockerfiles)
docker build images/python-base -t python-base

# Build venv-builder (tag must match FROM venv-builder in keystone Stage 1)
docker build images/venv-builder -t venv-builder
```

If the canonical `c5c3/...` tags are also desired, add them with `-t`:

```bash
docker build images/python-base -t python-base -t c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble
docker build images/venv-builder -t venv-builder -t c5c3/venv-builder:3.12-noble
```

<details><summary>Prompt To Fix With AI</summary>

`````markdown

Following these instructions exactly creates an image named `c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble` locally, then `docker build images/venv-builder` tries to resolve `FROM python-base` → Docker looks for `python-base:latest` which doesn't exist, causing the build to fail.

The same problem applies to the Keystone Stage 1 and Stage 2 (`FROM python-base`, `FROM venv-builder`).

The note correctly states "The tag names (`python-base`, `venv-builder`) must match the `FROM` directives" — but the preceding code block contradicts that. The commands need to use the bare tag names that the `FROM` directives reference:

```suggestion

# Build venv-builder (tag must match FROM venv-builder in keystone Stage 1)

```

If the canonical `c5c3/...` tags are also desired, add them with `-t`:

```bash

```
`````

code_comments.author: greptile-apps[bot]
code_comments.resolution: Fixed in container-images.md (Step 1: Build base images): changed primary build commands from -t c5c3/python-base:3.12-noble and -t c5c3/venv-builder:3.12-noble to bare -t python-base and -t venv-builder, added inline comments explaining the FROM directive match requirement, removed the misleading sentence about registry prefixes, and added a new 'canonical registry tags' code block showing dual -t usage — all matching the reviewer's suggestion.

resolution_summary: Both review comments were addressed with staged changes matching the reviewer's suggested fixes exactly. Comment [0] fixed the set -e exit code capture bug in verify_python_base.sh using the || exit_code=$? idiom. Comment [1] fixed the docker build tag names in container-images.md to use bare names matching FROM directives, and added a separate code block showing how to also apply canonical registry tags. No changes were made to files or locations beyond the two explicitly mentioned in the review.
